title: Sticky toffee cake
subtitle: The pub pudding in a sliceable, keep-all-week loaf
excerpt: The sticky toffee pudding baked as a loaf tin cake that holds its shape and keeps for days. The same Medjool date batter, the same toffee sauce, but portioned as a cake rather than spooned from a ramekin.
sourceNotes: Sticky toffee pudding became a fixture of British pub and restaurant menus from the 1970s, associated with the Udny Arms Hotel in Newburgh and later Francis Coulson and Brian Sack at the Sharrow Bay Hotel in Cumbria. The sliceable loaf-tin version reduces the pudding to its batter and sauce, making it more practical as a home cake. The date batter and toffee sauce have been reproduced widely; the recipe structure here follows the classic pub-kitchen version that entered common usage by the 1980s.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the cake without the sauce, well wrapped. Make the sauce fresh on the day of serving. Warm cake slices in a 160°C oven for 10 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake up to 3 days ahead and store in an airtight tin. Make the toffee sauce the day before and reheat gently.

glossaryTerms.definition: Large, soft, very sweet dates with a caramel-like flavour and a natural moisture content that produces a dense, sticky batter. They are the standard for sticky toffee pudding. Drier pressed dates can be used but need more soaking liquid to produce a comparable batter.

content.text: The cake version keeps where the pudding does not. Bake it on Sunday and it improves through the week, the date batter growing denser and the flavour deepening. The toffee sauce is not decoration; it is part of the recipe, poured over the warm cake when it comes out of the oven.
in a bowl, sprinkle over the bicarbonate of soda, and pour over the boiling water. Stir briefly and leave for 15 minutes until the dates are very soft and the water has turned dark brown.

Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Grease a 900 g loaf tin and line with baking paper. Beat the butter and sugar until combined. Beat in the eggs one at a time. Fold in the flour, then stir in the vanilla and the date mixture (liquid included), mashing the dates roughly with a fork as you go. The batter will be loose and dark. Pour into the tin and bake for 42 to 48 minutes until a skewer comes out clean, the top feels firm, and the edges have pulled from the tin. Leave in the tin on a wire rack.

Combine the butter, cream, and sugar in a saucepan over medium heat, stirring until the butter melts and the sugar dissolves. Bring to a gentle boil and cook for 3 to 4 minutes, stirring, until the sauce thickens slightly and coats the back of a spoon. While the cake is still in the tin, prick the top all over with a skewer and pour over half the sauce. Leave for 10 minutes to absorb, then turn out. Serve with the remaining sauce poured over each slice.
